summary: Joel observes that online services almost never include instructions for canceling accounts, treating it like a superstition that hiding cancellation will retain customers. He argues this is the result of misapplied A/B testing: companies can easily measure that telling people how to cancel increases cancellations, but they can't measure how many potential customers never sign up because they fear cancellation will be difficult. Joel shares Fog Creek's approach of offering unconditional money-back guarantees and easy cancellation, noting it has cost only 2% of revenue over six years.
key_points: FAQ pages for online services systematically omit cancellation instructions, as if hiding the option will prevent churn
A/B tests can measure the visible cost of easy cancellation but cannot measure the invisible cost of lost signups from customers who fear being trapped
This is a classic case of measuring benefit while ignoring cost — or vice versa — leading to poor business decisions
Fog Creek's unconditional 90-day money-back guarantee has cost only 2% of revenue, including chargebacks and fraud
Reducing perceived risk for new customers is more valuable than trapping existing ones
money_quotes: It's like they're all subject to the same bizarre superstition … if you don't tell people how to cancel, maybe they'll lose interest and keep paying you.
If you want to attract new customers, you have to give them the confidence that there is no risk in signing up or converting from whatever they were using before.
What's not easy to test is how many people never sign up in the first place because they're afraid that canceling is going to be a nightmare.
So what you have here is a somebody doing a cost/benefit analysis where they are measuring the benefit while ignoring the cost.
You may cancel at any time, on the Web, without hassle.
The moneyback guarantee has cost us precisely 2% of revenues, which also includes chargebacks, credit card fraud, and people who accidentally ordered twice.
I'm only measuring the cost, because the benefit is too hard to measure!
key_insight: Companies that hide cancellation options are optimizing based on measurable churn while ignoring the unmeasurable cost of potential customers who never sign up because they fear being trapped.
